attributes.stem:
A 54-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a two-day history of worsening left iliac fossa pain. He has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. He lives at home with his wife.

On examination, he appears well. His pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 135/90 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.2°C. There is some tenderness in the left iliac fossa, but the rest of the abdominal examination is normal. A CT scan confirms acute diverticulitis with some pericolic fat stranding and thickening of the bowel wall affecting a 10 cm segment of the sigmoid colon, where diverticulae are visible. No other abnormalities are detected. Investigations reveal a C-reactive protein of 14 mg/L (<8), with other laboratory values within normal range.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Analgesia and review in clinic in four days
Admission for intravenous antibiotics and observation
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with uncomplicated or simple diverticulitis, which describes inflammation without evidence of complications such as abscess, stricture, fistula, or perforation. Approximately 75% of diverticulitis cases are uncomplicated. In most circumstances, such patients can be managed at home.

Analgesia and review in clinic in four days is the most appropriate management for this low-risk patient with uncomplicated diverticulitis. Current guidelines recommend a clear liquid diet and outpatient follow-up. Antibiotics are not routinely necessary for low-risk cases and should be considered on a case-by-case basis. Those that might be considered for antibiotic therapy would include patients with substantial co-morbidities, signs of systemic infection, or immunosuppression.

Oral antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate) and outpatient review might be considered in certain cases of uncomplicated diverticulitis, such as in immunocompromised patients, those with significant comorbidities, or during pregnancy. However, for this low-risk patient, antibiotics are not necessary.

Admission for intravenous antibiotics and observation would be appropriate if the patient had limited home support, a fever exceeding 38.6°C, leucocytosis, complicated disease (based on CT assessment), significant comorbidities, or pain control issues. This patient does not meet these criteria.

Colonoscopy will be required at some stage to rule out other pathologies, but it should be delayed until the acute inflammatory episode has resolved, typically 6-8 weeks after the acute episode.

Laparoscopy and peritoneal washout is not indicated for uncomplicated diverticulitis. Surgical intervention is reserved for complicated cases or those not responding to conservative management.

In summary, uncomplicated diverticulitis in low-risk patients can often be managed conservatively on an outpatient basis. The focus should be on symptom management, dietary modifications, and appropriate follow-up. Antibiotics should be used selectively, and more invasive interventions are reserved for complicated cases or those not responding to initial management.
learningPoints: Most cases of uncomplicated diverticulitis can be managed on an outpatient basis.
Antibiotics are not routinely necessary for low-risk cases of uncomplicated diverticulitis.
Colonoscopy should be performed after resolution of the acute inflammatory episode in diverticulitis.
references: You H, Sweeny A, Cooper ML, Von Papen M, et al. The management of diverticulitis: a review of the guidelines. Med J Aust. 2019 Nov;211(9):421-427. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31352692/
Sacks OA, Hall J. Management of Diverticulitis A Review. JAMA Surg. 2024; 159:696-03.
Brown RF, Lopez K, Smith CB, et al. Diverticulitis A Review. JAMA 2025; July 24,
